description: Performance interpretations - athletic potential, exercise response, muscle composition, recovery

CC.interpretation: The CC (R/R) genotype retains alpha-actinin-3 expression. It is modestly enriched in some elite power-athlete cohorts, but it does not predict an individual's strength, speed, sport, or training response.
recommendations: Choose training from measured sprint, jump, strength, and endurance response
Treat this as interesting context rather than a talent result

CT.effect: One ACTN3 R allele and one X allele
CT.interpretation: The CT (R/X) genotype produces alpha-actinin-3 from one copy. Athlete-group associations are small and heterogeneous and cannot classify an individual as balanced, power-oriented, or endurance-oriented.
recommendations: Use repeatable performance tests and recovery data to guide training

TT.interpretation: The TT (X/X) genotype is associated with alpha-actinin-3 deficiency, a common non-disease state. It does not mean that your muscles are built for endurance or that power performance is impaired.
recommendations: Train for your goals and compare actual power and endurance benchmarks
Do not select a sport or supplement from this genotype

sources.label: ACTN3 athlete-status systematic review and meta-analysis

limitations: Athlete-status associations have modest effect sizes, study heterogeneity, ancestry differences, and possible publication bias.
A single genotype cannot predict performance, training response, injury, or the best sport for an individual.

AA.interpretation: Higher ACE activity - efficient blood pressure regulation during exercise
recommendations: Normal endurance potential
Standard response to training
Balanced response to both endurance and strength training

GG.interpretation: D allele associated with higher muscle efficiency, better endurance performance
recommendations: May have slight advantage in endurance events
Responds well to altitude training

CC.interpretation: The CC genotype corresponds to the coding-strand Gly/Gly genotype used in many studies. It has been associated with athlete status or aerobic-training response in some populations, but it does not measure mitochondrial function or reliably predict a larger VO2max response.
recommendations: Measure aerobic response across a consistent training block

CT.interpretation: The CT genotype corresponds to one Gly and one Ser coding-strand allele. Evidence does not support converting this result into a mitochondrial-efficiency label or a prescribed training volume.
recommendations: Compare measured VO2max or standardized field performance before and after training

TT.interpretation: The TT genotype corresponds to the coding-strand Ser/Ser genotype. Some small training studies reported a lower average aerobic response, but this does not establish mitochondrial impairment or individual non-response.
recommendations: Use an 8-12 week training block and objective performance measures before changing programming
Do not add supplements from this genotype alone

sources.label: PPARGC1A and human trainability systematic review

limitations: Training-response studies are small and differ in population, intervention, and outcome definition.
This genotype does not measure mitochondrial function, set achievable VO2max, or justify supplement use.

recommendations: Normal muscle building potential

AG.interpretation: One copy of K153R - slightly reduced myostatin inhibition
recommendations: May have slightly enhanced muscle growth response to training

GG.interpretation: G allele associated with lower myostatin - potential for greater muscle mass
recommendations: Natural advantage in muscle building
Responds well to resistance training

GG.interpretation: Standard vitamin D receptor activity
recommendations: Normal calcium absorption for bone and muscle

GA.interpretation: May have slightly altered vitamin D metabolism
recommendations: Ensure adequate vitamin D intake
Monitor vitamin D levels

AA.interpretation: May have reduced vitamin D sensitivity
recommendations: Higher vitamin D supplementation may be needed
Monitor bone health

TT.interpretation: Standard receptor function
recommendations: Normal vitamin D utilization
Monitor vitamin D status

CC.interpretation: May have reduced calcium absorption
recommendations: Ensure adequate vitamin D and calcium
Important for muscle function

TT.interpretation: Normal fat mobilization during exercise
recommendations: Standard fat utilization during training

TC.interpretation: Trp64Arg variant - reduced fat mobilization during exercise
recommendations: May have difficulty using fat for fuel during long exercise
Carb loading may help

CC.interpretation: Homozygous variant - significantly reduced fat mobilization
recommendations: Higher reliance on carbohydrates during exercise
Different pre-workout nutrition

GG.interpretation: No C34T loss-of-function allele was detected at this marker. This does not establish a particular exercise capacity or athlete profile.
recommendations: Use actual training response and performance tests to guide programming

AG.interpretation: One loss-of-function-associated allele was observed. Many carriers are asymptomatic; genotype alone does not explain fatigue or predict sprint, power, or endurance performance.
recommendations: Compare symptoms with workload, sleep, fueling, iron status, and measured recovery
Seek clinical evaluation for persistent or disproportionate exercise intolerance

AA.interpretation: Two loss-of-function-associated alleles can reduce skeletal-muscle AMP deaminase activity, but symptoms and exercise capacity vary. This result is not a diagnosis of metabolic myopathy.
recommendations: Discuss persistent exercise intolerance, cramps, or unusual fatigue with a qualified clinician
Use symptom-guided pacing while other common causes are assessed

limitations: Athlete-status enrichment is a group association and is not an individual performance test.
Penetrance and symptoms vary; fatigue and exercise intolerance have many genetic and non-genetic causes.

CC.interpretation: Standard insulin sensitivity and lipid metabolism
recommendations: Normal metabolic response to exercise

CT.interpretation: One copy - may have better metabolic adaptation to endurance training
recommendations: May respond well to endurance training
Standard approach to fitness

recommendations: Good metabolic fitness potential

recommendations: May benefit from anti-inflammatory recovery strategies

recommendations: Support mitochondrial function

recommendations: Aggressive mitochondrial support protocol
NAD+ precursors essential

Monitor mitochondrial markers
GG.theWhy: NRF1 variant affects mitochondrial gene expression
GG.scienceSimplified: Your mitochondrial energy production genes are less active

GG.interpretation: Standard bronchodilator response and fat mobilization
recommendations: Normal response to beta-2 agonists if used

AG.interpretation: Heterozygous - may have reduced bronchodilator response
recommendations: May benefit from pre-workout warm-up
Consider slightly longer warm-up for cardio

AA.interpretation: Homozygous - substantially reduced bronchodilator response and fat mobilization during exercise
recommendations: Extended warm-up essential
Consider inhaled beta-2 if prescribed
Longer cardio sessions may be more effective
Fat oxidation may be reduced during high intensity

CC.interpretation: Standard branched chain amino acid metabolism
recommendations: Standard protein intake sufficient

CG.interpretation: Heterozygous - may have slightly altered leucine/isoleucine/valine metabolism
recommendations: Ensure complete amino acid profile
Consider EAAs if plant-based diet

GG.interpretation: Homozygous - substantially altered branched chain amino acid metabolism
recommendations: Prioritize complete protein with all EAAs
Consider BCAA supplementation
Monitor for fatigue during training
More frequent protein intake

TT.effect: Two T alleles at COL5A1 rs12722
TT.interpretation: Some meta-analyses associate the TT genotype with tendon or ligament injury in selected cohorts, while results differ by ancestry, injury site, and study design. It does not measure tendon stiffness or predict that an injury will occur.
recommendations: Base load progression on training history, pain, biomechanics, and recovery
Use a clinician or physiotherapist for persistent tendon or ligament symptoms

CT.effect: One C allele and one T allele
CT.interpretation: Association studies do not support an individual intermediate-stiffness label. Training load, previous injury, biomechanics, sex, and sport are more actionable predictors.
recommendations: Use progressive loading and track symptoms regardless of genotype

CC.effect: Two C alleles at COL5A1 rs12722
CC.interpretation: The CC genotype is not a diagnosis of compliant tendons or high injury risk. Published associations are population-specific and sometimes directionally inconsistent across analyses.
recommendations: Use the same evidence-based load management and symptom monitoring as any athlete

limitations: Associations vary by ancestry, injury site, case definition, and genetic model.
This marker does not measure tissue mechanics or justify supplements, imaging, or a training restriction.

AA.effect: High MMP-1 expression — enhanced matrix metalloprotease activity
AA.interpretation: Elevated MMP-1 collagenase expression — potentially faster tissue remodeling and collagen turnover; associated with higher exercise-induced connective tissue adaptation rate but also higher injury susceptibility in acute load spikes
recommendations: Gradual load progression critical — allow connective tissue remodeling to catch up with muscular adaptation
Collagen 10-15g + vitamin C pre-training for substrate support
Anti-inflammatory recovery: omega-3, tart cherry

GA.interpretation: Intermediate collagenase activity — moderate tissue remodeling rate
recommendations: Standard progressive loading and collagen support

GG.interpretation: Normal matrix metalloprotease-1 activity — standard connective tissue remodeling rate
recommendations: Standard training progression principles apply

TT.interpretation: Reduced growth differentiation factor 5 — lower cartilage and tendon cell proliferation response; associated with increased osteoarthritis risk and joint space narrowing, particularly knee OA
recommendations: Glucosamine sulfate 1500mg daily (most evidence-backed cartilage support supplement)
Low-impact training options (cycling, swimming) to preserve joint cartilage
Maintain BMI < 25 — excess weight accelerates cartilage loss with this genotype
Collagen peptides 10-15g daily for synovial fluid support

TC.interpretation: One low-expression allele — mild joint resilience reduction
recommendations: Glucosamine and low-impact cross-training beneficial

CC.interpretation: Normal cartilage and ligament growth factor signaling — standard joint resilience
recommendations: Standard joint health practices

CC.effect: Higher nNOS expression — elevated neuronal NO
CC.interpretation: Increased neuronal nitric oxide synthase — higher central NO production affecting muscle fatigue signaling, reaction time, and post-exercise recovery rate
recommendations: Aerobic training benefits enhanced by high nNOS activity (NO drives mitochondrial biogenesis)
L-citrulline or dietary nitrates may further amplify performance (synergistic with high nNOS)
Standard NO-supporting strategies

TT.interpretation: Standard neuronal NO production — normal fatigue signaling
recommendations: Dietary nitrates (beetroot, arugula) beneficial for endurance performance

GG.interpretation: Beta-2 adrenergic receptor Gly16 — greater receptor downregulation with prolonged agonist stimulation; relevant for bronchodilator response in asthma and sympathomimetic response during exercise
recommendations: If using beta-2 agonist inhalers (asthma): tolerance develops faster; discuss step-up therapy with physician
High-intensity interval training may be less stimulant-dependent for performance due to receptor sensitivity
Caffeine (indirect sympathomimetic) may have attenuated ergogenic effect compared to Arg16 individuals
Standard ergogenic supplementation guidelines apply

AA.interpretation: Slower receptor downregulation — more sustained beta-2 agonist response; better long-term bronchodilator response and potentially greater catecholamine effect during sustained exercise
recommendations: Caffeine ergogenic effect may be more pronounced (sustained receptor availability)
Consider regular versus as-needed inhaler strategy with physician if asthmatic (Arg16 shows better sustained response to LABA)

TT.effect: Higher UCP3 expression — increased mitochondrial uncoupling
TT.interpretation: Higher uncoupling protein 3 in skeletal muscle — greater thermogenic capacity, potentially higher fat oxidation at rest, but reduced exercise economy (some energy 'wasted' as heat rather than ATP)
recommendations: Endurance training benefits from higher fat oxidation efficiency in this genotype
Ensure adequate dietary fat for fuel — very low fat diets may impair performance
Higher caloric needs due to uncoupling thermogenesis — do not under-fuel
Standard endurance training nutrition

CC.effect: Lower UCP3 expression — tighter mitochondrial coupling
CC.interpretation: Lower muscle uncoupling protein 3 — more efficient mitochondrial ATP production; better metabolic economy during sustained exercise
recommendations: Leverage the metabolic efficiency advantage in prolonged aerobic events

GG.effect: Higher VEGFB — enhanced cardiac angiogenesis with exercise
GG.interpretation: Elevated vascular endothelial growth factor B — greater exercise-induced coronary angiogenesis and cardiac capillary density; the 'trained heart' adapts better with GG genotype
recommendations: Aerobic training especially beneficial for this genotype — coronary reserve expands more
Zone 2 training (conversational pace aerobic) maximizes VEGFB-driven cardiac capillary adaptation

AG.interpretation: Moderate cardiac angiogenic response to exercise
recommendations: Regular aerobic training for cardiovascular adaptation

AA.effect: Lower VEGFB — less cardiac angiogenic response to training
AA.interpretation: Reduced VEGFB-mediated cardiac vascularization with exercise training
recommendations: High volume aerobic training compensates; consistent Zone 2 is critical
Nitrate-rich diet (beet root, leafy greens) for alternate vascular growth signaling

TT.effect: T/T — significantly lower bone mineral density
TT.interpretation: COL1A1 Sp1 TT variant — ~2x greater risk for osteoporosis and stress fractures; reduced type I collagen α1 chain quality leads to structurally weaker bone and tendon
recommendations: DEXA bone density scan at age 40 (earlier than standard guideline)
Calcium 1200mg daily from food preferably (dairy, leafy greens, fortified foods)
Vitamin D3 3000IU targeting 50-70 ng/mL (critical for calcium absorption)
Weight-bearing exercise mandatory: resistance training 3x/week
Collagen peptides 15g daily (stimulates osteoblast collagen synthesis)

GT.interpretation: One T allele — moderate COL1A1 effect on bone mineral density and fracture risk
recommendations: Vitamin D3, calcium, weight-bearing exercise and collagen supplementation recommended

GG.effect: G/G — normal type I collagen quality
GG.interpretation: Wild-type COL1A1 — standard bone mineral density and connective tissue strength
recommendations: Standard calcium, vitamin D, and weight-bearing exercise

TT.effect: Common genotype — standard heel bone mineral density

recommendations: No action needed; standard heel bone mineral density management applies

CT.effect: One SMG6 protective allele — strong heel bone mineral density benefit
CT.interpretation: One copy of the GWAS-identified protective allele for heel bone mineral density. Strong benefit observed in population studies (n=426,824, p=6e-143)
recommendations: One protective SMG6 allele — generally favorable

CC.effect: Homozygous SMG6 protective allele — maximum heel bone mineral density benefit
CC.interpretation: Homozygous for the GWAS protective allele at SMG6. Strongest favorable association with heel bone mineral density
recommendations: Favorable SMG6 variant — continue healthy lifestyle to maximise this genetic advantage
